event: The first human hunter-gatherers settle the emerging Aland archipelago as land rises from the sea following the last Ice Age.
Stone Age communities of the Comb Ceramic and later Pitted Ware cultures inhabit the islands, leaving seal-hunting and pottery sites.
Bronze Age settlers establish coastal sites and burial cairns, linking the islands to wider Scandinavian trade networks.
Iron Age settlement intensifies, with hundreds of grave fields and the distinctive clay paw burial rite appearing across the islands.
During the Viking Age the islands lie along key Baltic trade and raiding routes, with silver hoards and fortified sites attesting to prosperity.
Population on the islands declines sharply for reasons still debated, leaving large areas seemingly depopulated for a time.
Swedish-speaking settlers repopulate Aland as it is gradually drawn into the Kingdom of Sweden and Christianized.
Construction of stone churches such as those at Jomala and Sund reflects Aland's firm integration into Catholic Sweden.
Construction begins on Kastelholm Castle, which becomes the administrative center of the islands under the Swedish crown.
Tax records show Aland with a settled farming and fishing population firmly governed as part of Sweden's Abo (Turku) region.
Duke Charles besieges and partly burns Kastelholm Castle during the Swedish war of succession against Sigismund.
Russian forces occupy Aland during the Great Northern War's 'Greater Wrath,' forcing much of the population to flee to Sweden.
Swedish and Russian envoys hold the Aland Congress peace negotiations on the islands during the Great Northern War.
The Treaty of Nystad ends the Great Northern War, returning Aland to Sweden after years of Russian occupation.
The Treaty of Fredrikshamn cedes Aland, along with Finland, from Sweden to the Russian Empire after the Finnish War.
Russia begins building the massive Bomarsund fortress to project naval power in the Baltic from Aland.
During the Crimean War, an Anglo-French fleet bombards and captures the Bomarsund fortress, then demolishes it.
The Treaty of Paris demilitarizes Aland, banning Russia from fortifying the islands.
The town of Mariehamn is founded as Aland's capital and named after the Russian Empress Maria Alexandrovna.
Mariehamn becomes a hub for the world's last great fleet of windjammer sailing ships under shipowner Gustaf Erikson.
Amid the Russian Revolution and Finland's independence, Alanders petition to reunite the Swedish-speaking islands with Sweden.
Swedish and German troops intervene on Aland during the Finnish Civil War, heightening the dispute over the islands' future.
Finland's parliament passes the first Act on the Autonomy of Aland, granting the islands self-governing status.
The League of Nations awards sovereignty over Aland to Finland while guaranteeing autonomy, Swedish language, and demilitarization.
The Aland Convention reaffirms the islands' neutralization and demilitarization under international guarantee.
Aland holds its first Landsting (parliament) elections and inaugurates its self-government on June 9.
Finland and Sweden's plan to jointly refortify Aland (the Stockholm Plan) collapses amid pre-World War II tensions.
Aland remains demilitarized and largely untouched as Finland exits World War II under the Moscow Armistice.
A revised Act on the Autonomy of Aland strengthens and modernizes the islands' self-government within Finland.
Aland adopts its own blue, gold, and red flag, symbolizing its distinct identity.
Aland gains its own membership in the Nordic Council, recognizing its autonomous status in Nordic cooperation.
Aland begins issuing its own postage stamps, a further mark of its autonomy.
A new Autonomy Act takes effect in 1993, broadening Aland's legislative powers and confirming its special status.
Aland joins the European Union together with Finland but secures special exemptions, including duty-free sales and a tax border.
Aland's parliament gains a role in EU affairs and the right to be heard, reflected in arrangements around the Lisbon Treaty.
Aland celebrates the centenary of its autonomy and the League of Nations' landmark settlement of the Aland question.
Russia's invasion of Ukraine reignites debate over Aland's demilitarized and neutralized status in a tense Baltic security environment.
